<% content_for :title, "" %> <% # Wave 2 Commit 3b — family step writes to Marriage + Parentage rows. # Form fields use direct names (relationship_kind, partner[...], # children[i][...]) instead of being scoped to a single model. The # OnboardingController#update_family transactionally orchestrates # sync_partner! + sync_children!. current_kind = @active_marriage&.kind || params[:relationship_kind].presence has_children = (@children_params.any? || params[:has_children] == "yes") ? "yes" : params[:has_children] children_for_form = @children_params if has_children == "yes" && children_for_form.empty? children_for_form = [{ "id" => nil, "position" => 0, "capacity_status" => "under-18", "disabled_answer" => nil, "lacks_mental_capacity_answer" => nil, "co_parent_type" => nil }] end active_partner = @active_marriage&.partner_person partner_first_name = active_partner&.first_name || params.dig(:partner, :first_name) partner_last_name = active_partner&.last_name || params.dig(:partner, :last_name) partner_started_at = @active_marriage&.started_at || params.dig(:partner, :started_at) %> <%= form_with url: mobile_onboarding_family_path, method: :patch, class: "mobile-screen mobile-screen--onboarding", html: { data: { controller: "family-form", action: "change->family-form#formChange click->family-form#formClick focusin->family-form#formFocusin input->family-form#formInput", family_form_user_last_name_value: @person&.last_name.to_s, family_form_partner_first_name_value: partner_first_name.to_s, family_form_relationship_kind_value: current_kind.to_s } } do |form| %> <% partner_fields_body = capture do %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/form_row", label: "First name", for_id: "partner_first_name", body: capture { text_field_tag "partner[first_name]", partner_first_name, class: "mobile-input", id: "partner_first_name", autocomplete: "off", data: { role: "spouse-first-name" } } %> <%= render "mobile/shared/form_row", label: "Last name", for_id: "partner_last_name", body: capture { text_field_tag "partner[last_name]", partner_last_name, class: "mobile-input", id: "partner_last_name", autocomplete: "off", data: { role: "spouse-last-name", prefilled: (partner_last_name.present? && partner_last_name == @person&.last_name).to_s } } %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/date_field", field_name: "partner[started_at]", id_prefix: "partner_started_at", label: "Wedding date", hint: nil, value: partner_started_at, placeholder: "Select date", min: (Date.current - 100.years), max: Date.current, picker_title: "Wedding date", data_role: "partner-started-at" %> <% end %> <% children_section_body = capture do %>
<% children_for_form.each_with_index do |child, index| %> <%= render "child_fields", child: child, child_index: index, index: index, allow_remove: children_for_form.size > 1, partnered: current_user.has_partner?, partner_first_name: current_user.partner_display_name %> <% end %>
<%# Hidden by default — Stimulus reveals once Child 1's first_name has any content (or whenever there's already more than one card). %> <% end %> <%= render "mobile/shared/screen_header", step_number: 3, total_steps: 5 %> <%= render "mobile/shared/form_errors", record: @person %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/hero_block", title: "Your family.", subtitle: "This is where the flow becomes more personal. We keep it on one calm page so the whole picture stays visible." %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/choice_group", field_name: "relationship_kind", label: "Which best describes your current situation?", value: current_kind, options: [["Single", "single"], ["Married", "married"], ["Civil partnership", "civil_partnership"], ["Cohabiting", "cohabiting"], ["Widowed", "widowed"]], id_prefix: "relationship_kind", collapsible: true, data_role: "relationship-status" %>
> <%= render "mobile/shared/grouped_section", title: "Partner details", subtle: true, body: partner_fields_body %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/form_row", label: "How many times have you been divorced?", for_id: "times_divorced", body: capture { number_field_tag "times_divorced", @person&.times_divorced.to_i, min: 0, step: 1, class: "mobile-input mobile-input--narrow", data: { role: "times-divorced" } } %>
> <%= render "mobile/shared/form_row", label: "How many times have you been widowed?", for_id: "times_widowed", body: capture { number_field_tag "times_widowed", @person&.times_widowed.to_i, min: 0, step: 1, class: "mobile-input mobile-input--narrow", data: { role: "times-widowed" } } %>
<%= render "mobile/shared/choice_group", field_name: "has_children", label: "Do you have children or guardianship responsibilities?", value: has_children, options: [["Yes", "yes"], ["No", "no"]], id_prefix: "has_children", collapsible: true, data_role: "has-children" %>
> <%= render "mobile/shared/grouped_section", title: "Tell us about those in your care", body: children_section_body %>
<%= form.submit "Continue", class: "mobile-button", data: { controller: "haptic", haptic_style_value: "medium" } %>
<% end %>

def family_params
      base = params.permit(:relationship_kind, :times_divorced, :times_widowed, :has_children)
      partner = params.fetch(:partner, {}).permit(:first_name, :last_name, :started_at)
      raw_children = params.fetch(:children, {})
      raw_children = raw_children.to_unsafe_h if raw_children.respond_to?(:to_unsafe_h)
      children = (raw_children || {}).each_with_object({}) do |(index, child_params), h|
        next unless child_params.respond_to?(:[])
        permitted_params = child_params.respond_to?(:permit) ? child_params : ActionController::Parameters.new(child_params.to_h)
        h[index] = permitted_params.permit(

        )
      end
      base.merge(partner: partner, children: children)
    end

content.content:
module CookieTestHelper
  def write_signed_cookie(key, value)
    ActionDispatch::TestRequest.create.cookie_jar.tap do |jar|
      jar.signed[key] = value
      cookies[key.to_s] = jar[key]
    end
  end

  def read_signed_cookie(key)
    raw = cookies[key.to_s]
    return nil if raw.blank?

    jar = ActionDispatch::TestRequest.create.cookie_jar
    jar[key] = raw
    jar.signed[key]
  end
end

ActiveSupport.on_load(:action_dispatch_integration_test) do
  include CookieTestHelper
end

content.text: The test helper needs to seed both `OnboardingSession` (for legacy intro/completed gates) and `User` + `Person` (+ `Marriage` + `Parentage`) for the new shape. Extending it now:

input.old_string:
private

    def create_onboarding_session(attributes = {})
      onboarding_session = OnboardingSession.create!({ token: SecureRandom.hex(24) }.merge(attributes))
      write_signed_cookie(OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY, onboarding_session.token)
      onboarding_session
    end
  end
end
input.new_string:
private

    PARTNER_KIND_TRANSLATION = {
      "married"           => "married",
      "civil-partnership" => "civil_partnership",
      "cohabiting"        => "cohabiting"
    }.freeze

    PARENTAGE_KIND_BY_FORM_VALUE = {
      "biological-child" => "biological",
      "adopted-child"    => "adoptive",
      "stepchild"        => "step",
      "foster-child"     => "foster"
    }.freeze

    PERSON_IDENTITY_KEYS = %i[first_name middle_names last_name date_of_birth
      country_of_residence nationality domiciled_in_uk currently_resident_in_uk
      parents_alive parents_in_law_alive siblings_alive number_of_siblings
      times_divorced times_widowed].freeze

    # Seeds both a legacy OnboardingSession (for intro/EntryRouting gates) and
    # the matching User + Person + Marriage + Parentage rows the new shape
    # reads from. Same attribute hash drives both — translates relationship_status
    # and children_payload into typed rows.
    def create_onboarding_session(attributes = {})
      attrs = attributes.dup

      onboarding_session = OnboardingSession.create!({ token: SecureRandom.hex(24) }.merge(attrs))
      write_signed_cookie(OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY, onboarding_session.token)

      user = User.create!(
        intro_seen_at: attrs[:intro_seen_at],
        video_completed_at: attrs[:video_completed_at],
        questionnaire_completed_at: attrs[:questionnaire_completed_at]
      )
      write_signed_cookie(:user_token, user.token)

      if attrs.slice(*PERSON_IDENTITY_KEYS).any? { |_, v| v.present? }
        person_attrs = attrs.slice(*PERSON_IDENTITY_KEYS).compact
        person = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "self", **person_attrs)
        user.update!(will_maker_person: person)
      end

      kind = PARTNER_KIND_TRANSLATION[attrs[:relationship_status]]
      if kind && attrs[:spouse_first_name].present?
        spouse = user.people.create!(
          relationship_kind: "spouse",
          first_name: attrs[:spouse_first_name],
          last_name: attrs[:spouse_last_name]
        )
        Marriage.create!(
          will_maker_person: user.will_maker_person,
          partner_person: spouse,
          kind: kind,
          phase: "active",
          started_at: attrs[:partner_started_at]
        )
      end

      if attrs[:has_children] == "yes" && attrs[:children_payload].present?
        attrs[:children_payload].each_with_index do |child, idx|
          c_person = user.people.create!(
            relationship_kind: "child",
            first_name: child["first_name"],
            last_name: child["last_name"],
            date_of_birth: child["date_of_birth"]
          )
          Parentage.create!(
            parent_person: user.will_maker_person,
            child_person: c_person,
            kind: PARENTAGE_KIND_BY_FORM_VALUE[child["relationship"]] || "biological",
            position: idx
          )
        end
      end

      onboarding_session
    end
  end
end

test "family persists child capacity flags and co-parent answer; redirects to extended family" do
      create_onboarding_session(
        video_intro_version: 1,
        video_completed_at: Time.current,
        intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke",
        last_name: "Aikman",
        date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england",
        nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
        currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0",
        times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "yes",
        children: {
          "0" => {
            position: "0",
            first_name: "Alex",
            last_name: "Aikman",
            relationship: "biological-child",
            disabled_answer: "no",
            lacks_mental_capacity_answer: "no",
            co_parent_type: "no_sole"

      assert_equal false, child.lacks_mental_capacity
      # co_parent_type "no_sole" → no second Parentage
      assert_equal 1, Parentage.where(child_person: child).count
    end

        video_intro_version: 1,
        video_completed_at: Time.current,
        intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke",
        last_name: "Aikman",
        date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england",
        nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
        currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "cohabiting",
        partner: { first_name: "Sam", last_name: "Partner" },  # no started_at
        times_divorced: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_response :unprocessable_entity
      assert_match(/required when cohabiting/i, response.body)
    end

    test "family accepts cohabiting submission with partner_started_at" do
      create_onboarding_session(
        video_intro_version: 1,
        video_completed_at: Time.current,
        intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke",
        last_name: "Aikman",
        date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england",
        nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
        currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "cohabiting",
        partner: { first_name: "Sam", last_name: "Partner", started_at: "2020-06-15" },
        times_divorced: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = User.where(email_address: nil).order(:created_at).last
      assert_equal Date.new(2020, 6, 15), user.active_marriage.started_at
    end

    test "family persists times_divorced + times_widowed counters on the will-maker Person" do
      create_onboarding_session(
        video_intro_version: 1,
        video_completed_at: Time.current,
        intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke",
        last_name: "Aikman",
        date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england",
        nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
        currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "2",
        times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = User.where(email_address: nil).order(:created_at).last
      assert_equal 2, user.will_maker_person.times_divorced
      assert_equal 0, user.will_maker_person.times_widowed
    end

require "application_system_test_case"

module Mobile
  class FamilyStepConditionalRevealTest < ApplicationSystemTestCase
    test "selecting Married reveals the partner-fields section, Yes-children reveals children" do
      # Start without an active marriage / children so the choice-groups render
      # uncollapsed (Capybara can't click options inside an is-collapsed group).
      seed_user_minimum

      visit_family_path

      # Initially: partner fields hidden, children section hidden
      assert_selector "[data-role='partner-fields']", visible: :hidden
      assert_selector "[data-role='children-section']", visible: :hidden

      # Pick Married — partner fields should appear
      choose "Married", allow_label_click: true, visible: :all
      assert_selector "[data-role='partner-fields']", visible: :visible

      # Pick Yes for has-children — children section should appear with at least one child card
      within "[data-role='has-children']" do
        choose "Yes", allow_label_click: true, visible: :all
      end
      assert_selector "[data-role='children-section']", visible: :visible
      assert_selector "[data-child-card]", minimum: 1
    end

    test "co-parent radio shows partner-name option only when partnered, with sub-field reveals" do
      user = seed_user_minimum
      will_maker = user.will_maker_person

      # Active marriage with Sarah
      spouse = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "spouse", first_name: "Sarah", last_name: "Aikman")
      Marriage.create!(
        will_maker_person: will_maker,
        partner_person: spouse,
        kind: "married",
        phase: "active"
      )

      # One existing child Charlie (no co-parent picked yet)
      charlie = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "child", first_name: "Charlie", last_name: "Aikman")
      Parentage.create!(parent_person: will_maker, child_person: charlie, kind: "biological", position: 0)

      visit_family_path

      # When partnered, the "Yes, with Sarah" option is present (smart partner label)
      assert_selector "input[value='yes_with_partner']", visible: :all
      assert_match "Yes, with Sarah", page.html

      # Sub-pickers start hidden
      assert_selector "[data-role='co-parent-partner-rel']", visible: :hidden
      assert_selector "[data-role='co-parent-other-fields']", visible: :hidden

      # Pick "Yes, with Sarah" — partner-rel sub-picker reveals
      within "[data-role='co-parent-type']" do
        choose "Yes, with Sarah", allow_label_click: true, visible: :all
      end
      assert_selector "[data-role='co-parent-partner-rel']", visible: :visible
      assert_selector "[data-role='co-parent-other-fields']", visible: :hidden
    end

    test "Add another button hidden until Child 1 first_name has content" do
      user = seed_user_minimum
      will_maker = user.will_maker_person

      # One unnamed child (so the empty form row renders, but no first_name yet)
      empty_child = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "child")
      Parentage.create!(parent_person: will_maker, child_person: empty_child, kind: "biological", position: 0)

      visit_family_path

      # Add-another button is hidden initially — single empty child, "another" is misleading.
      assert_no_selector "[data-add-child]"

      first_card = find("[data-child-card]", match: :first)
      first_card.find("[data-role='child-first-name']").set("Charlie")

      assert_selector "[data-add-child]"
    end

    private

    # Build the minimum scaffolding the family step needs:
    # - OnboardingSession (still gating intro_seen + EntryRouting in the controller)
    # - User with the matching cookie + a will-maker Person filled out for welcome+location.
    def seed_user_minimum
      onboarding_session = OnboardingSession.create!(
        token: SecureRandom.hex(24),
        video_intro_version: 1, video_completed_at: Time.current, intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke", last_name: "Aikman", date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england", nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes", currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )

      user = User.create!(intro_seen_at: Time.current)
      person = user.people.create!(
        relationship_kind: "self",
        first_name: "Luke", last_name: "Aikman", date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england", nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes", currently_resident_in_uk: "yes"
      )
      user.update!(will_maker_person: person)

      visit "/"
      add_signed_browser_cookie(OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY, onboarding_session.token)
      add_signed_browser_cookie(:user_token, user.token)

      user
    end

    def visit_family_path
      visit mobile_onboarding_family_path
    end

    def add_signed_browser_cookie(name, value)
      jar = ActionDispatch::TestRequest.create.cookie_jar
      jar.signed[name] = value
      page.driver.browser.manage.add_cookie(
        name: name.to_s,
        value: jar[name],
        path: "/"
      )
    end
  end
end

content.text: Add new tests for Marriage / Parentage shape per v2-final 3b.4:

input.old_string: test "extended family requires partner parents answer for cohabiting users" do
input.new_string:
# === Wave 2 Commit 3b — Marriage + Parentage shape ===

    test "update_family married creates a spouse Person and active Marriage row" do
      seed_welcome_complete

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "married",
        partner: { first_name: "Sarah", last_name: "Aikman" },
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = anonymous_user
      assert user.active_marriage.present?, "active_marriage should be set"
      assert_equal "married", user.active_marriage.kind
      assert_equal "Sarah", user.active_marriage.partner_person.first_name
      assert_equal "spouse", user.active_marriage.partner_person.relationship_kind
    end

    test "update_family transitioning married -> single ends the active Marriage" do
      create_onboarding_session(
        video_intro_version: 1, video_completed_at: Time.current, intro_seen_at: Time.current,
        first_name: "Luke", last_name: "Aikman", date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
        country_of_residence: "england", nationality: "british",
        domiciled_in_uk: "yes", currently_resident_in_uk: "yes",
        relationship_status: "married", spouse_first_name: "Sarah", spouse_last_name: "Aikman",
        has_children: "no"
      )
      assert anonymous_user.active_marriage.present?, "test setup should seed an active marriage"

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = anonymous_user
      assert_nil user.active_marriage, "active marriage should be ended"
      ended = user.will_maker_person.marriages_as_will_maker.first
      assert_equal "separated", ended.phase
      assert_equal Date.current, ended.ended_at
    end

    test "update_family yes_with_other creates an ad-hoc co_parent Person + second Parentage" do
      seed_welcome_complete

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "yes",
        children: {
          "0" => {
            position: "0",
            first_name: "Charlie", last_name: "Aikman",
            relationship: "biological-child",
            disabled_answer: "no", lacks_mental_capacity_answer: "no",
            co_parent_type: "yes_with_other",
            co_parent_other_first_name: "Dawn", co_parent_other_last_name: "Other",
            co_parent_other_relationship_to_child: "biological"
          }
        }
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = anonymous_user
      child = user.will_maker_person.children_via_parentage.first
      assert_equal "Charlie", child.first_name

      parentages = Parentage.where(child_person: child)
      assert_equal 2, parentages.count, "expected will-maker + co-parent Parentage rows"
      co_parent = parentages.find { |p| p.parent_person.relationship_kind == "co_parent" }.parent_person
      assert_equal "Dawn", co_parent.first_name
    end

    test "update_family removing a child destroys child Person and cleans up orphaned co_parent" do
      seed_welcome_complete
      # First request: create child + ad-hoc co-parent
      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "yes",
        children: {
          "0" => {
            position: "0",
            first_name: "Charlie", last_name: "Aikman",
            relationship: "biological-child",
            disabled_answer: "no", lacks_mental_capacity_answer: "no",
            co_parent_type: "yes_with_other",
            co_parent_other_first_name: "Dawn", co_parent_other_last_name: "Other",
            co_parent_other_relationship_to_child: "biological"
          }
        }
      }
      assert_response :redirect
      user = anonymous_user
      assert_equal 2, user.people.where(relationship_kind: %w[child co_parent]).count

      # Second request: user removes children entirely
      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user.reload
      assert_equal 0, user.will_maker_person.children_via_parentage.count
      # Orphaned co-parent should be destroyed too.
      assert_equal 0, user.people.where(relationship_kind: "co_parent").count
    end

    test "extended family requires partner parents answer for cohabiting users" do

      assert_equal "spouse", user.active_marriage.partner_person.relationship_kind
    end

        video_intro_version: 1, video_completed_at: Time.current, intro_seen_at: Time.current,

        has_children: "no"
      )
      assert anonymous_user.active_marriage.present?, "test setup should seed an active marriage"

      patch mobile_onboarding_family_path, params: {
        relationship_kind: "single",
        times_divorced: "0", times_widowed: "0",
        has_children: "no"
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = anonymous_user
      assert_nil user.active_marriage, "active marriage should be ended"
      ended = user.will_maker_person.marriages_as_will_maker.first
      assert_equal "separated", ended.phase
      assert_equal Date.current, ended.ended_at
    end

            co_parent_other_relationship_to_child: "biological"
          }
        }
      }

      assert_redirected_to mobile_onboarding_extended_family_path
      user = anonymous_user
      child = user.will_maker_person.children_via_parentage.first
      assert_equal "Charlie", child.first_name

      parentages = Parentage.where(child_person: child)
      assert_equal 2, parentages.count, "expected will-maker + co-parent Parentage rows"
      co_parent = parentages.find { |p| p.parent_person.relationship_kind == "co_parent" }.parent_person
      assert_equal "Dawn", co_parent.first_name
    end

git add app/controllers/mobile/onboarding_controller.rb app/javascript/controllers/family_form_controller.js app/models/ app/views/mobile/onboarding/ test/ && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Wave 2 Commit 3b: family step writes to Marriage + Parentage rows

OnboardingController#update_family now orchestrates Marriage + Parentage
writes via sync_partner! / sync_children! / sync_co_parent! controller-
level helpers. After this commit OnboardingController has zero references
to OnboardingSession — Commit 5 drops the legacy table.

User+Person are the canonical sources; OnboardingSession is dead-but-not-
yet-buried (Mobile::EntryRouting + intro_seen gate still touch it).

Highlights:

- User: has_partner? / partner_possessive_label / partner_display_name /
  partner_kind_fallback all derive from active_marriage. Drops the prior
  spouse_person association in favour of one source of truth.
- Person: :family_step validation context for spouse / child / co_parent
  first_name + last_name presence.
- Marriage: started_at_required_when_cohabiting validation (cohabitation
  start is legally relevant; married rows record but don't require dates).
- OnboardingController#update_family: transactional, with rescue branch
  that reloads current_user + builds @children_params from form params
  for re-render after validation failure (B4 / G4 from QA).
  - sync_partner! creates / updates / ends Marriage + spouse Person
  - sync_children! upserts child Persons + their will-maker Parentage,
    delegates co-parent shape, destroys children removed from form
  - sync_co_parent! handles yes_with_partner / yes_with_other /
    no_deceased / no_sole transitions, captures prior parent_person
    before update so orphan check works correctly (B10)
- Constant lookup PARENTAGE_KIND_BY_FORM_VALUE replaces a method (E5).
- family.html.erb rewritten with direct field names: relationship_kind,
  partner[first_name], partner[last_name], partner[started_at],
  times_divorced, times_widowed, has_children, children[i][...].
  Form binds to no model — orchestration is on the controller side.
- _child_fields.html.erb fields renamed children[i][...]; hidden
  position field for stable ordering across re-renders.
- extended_family.html.erb now reads current_user.has_partner? /
  current_user.partner_possessive_label (was @onboarding_session).
- Stimulus family_form_controller.js selectors updated:
  onboarding_session[children_payload][i][...] → children[i][...]
  onboarding_session[spouse_*]                  → partner[*]
  PARTNERED_KINDS / PARTNER_DATE_LABELS swap "civil-partnership"
  hyphen for Marriage::KINDS underscore form.

Tests:
- 4 new controller tests: married creates Marriage, married→single ends
  it, yes_with_other creates ad-hoc co_parent + 2nd Parentage, removing
  children cleans up orphaned co_parent rows.
- create_onboarding_session helper extended to also seed User + Person +
  Marriage + Parentage from the same attribute hash, so existing
  family-step tests work against the new shape.
- System tests rewritten to seed User+Person directly instead of OS-only.

147 unit (was 143), 3 system, all green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Wave 2 Commit 3b shipped as `f74fd95`. Recap:

**Family step now writes typed rows** — `Marriage` for partnerships, `Parentage` for parent-child links, fresh `Person` rows for spouse / children / ad-hoc co-parents. After this commit `OnboardingController` has **zero references** to `OnboardingSession`.

**Controller orchestration** (no fat-model methods — Plan-forbidden):
- `sync_partner!` — creates/updates/ends Marriage + spouse Person
- `sync_children!` — upserts child Person + Parentage, removes children no longer in form (cascade-delete)
- `sync_co_parent!` — handles `yes_with_partner` / `yes_with_other` / `no_deceased` / `no_sole`, cleans up orphaned ad-hoc co_parent rows when type changes
- Single transaction; rescue branch reloads `current_user` and rebuilds `@children_params` from form params for re-render

**User cleanup**:
- `User#has_partner?` + `partner_possessive_label` + `partner_display_name` now derive from `active_marriage` (one source of truth — dropped the `spouse_person` shortcut)

**Stimulus** — selector audit: `onboarding_session[children_payload][i][...]` → `children[i][...]`; `partner[*]` everywhere; `civil-partnership` (hyphen) → `civil_partnership` (underscore) to match `Marriage::KINDS`.

**Tests** — 147 unit (was 143), 3 system, all green:
- 4 new controller tests cover married creation, married→single transition (active marriage ends, phase=separated), `yes_with_other` co-parent ad-hoc creation, removed-children cascade with orphaned-co_parent cleanup
- `create_onboarding_session` helper now seeds both legacy + new shape; system tests rewritten to seed `User+Person` directly

You can now exercise the full onboarding end-to-end in browser — every step writes to the new shape. Once you've verified, ready for Commit 5 (drop `onboarding_sessions` table + reap dead `Mobile::BaseController` machinery)? Commit 4 (sessions + auth + login warning) can wait until you actually need it.

message.content: Do we have a destroy method on User (so if I destroy an old user in motoradmin, associated sessions and persons, etc, will be deleted with them)?

content.text: Good catch. Let me check what happens — the `dependent: :destroy` declarations are in place, but the FK from `users.will_maker_person_id → people.id` could cause an ordering issue when the will-maker Person gets destroyed before the User row itself. Quick test:

input.command:
RAILS_ENV=test bin/rails runner '
ActiveRecord::Base.transaction do
  user = User.create!
  person = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "self", first_name: "Luke", last_name: "Aikman")
  user.update!(will_maker_person: person)
  spouse = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "spouse", first_name: "Sarah")
  Marriage.create!(will_maker_person: person, partner_person: spouse, kind: "married", phase: "active")
  child = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "child", first_name: "C")
  Parentage.create!(parent_person: person, child_person: child, kind: "biological")

  puts "Before destroy:"
  puts "  user: #{user.id}, persons: #{user.people.count}, marriages: #{Marriage.count}, parentages: #{Parentage.count}"

  begin
    user.destroy!
    puts "After destroy:"
    puts "  user persisted? #{user.persisted?}"
    puts "  persons: #{Person.count}, marriages: #{Marriage.count}, parentages: #{Parentage.count}, wills: #{Will.count}"
  rescue => e
    puts "ERROR: #{e.class}: #{e.message[0..200]}"
  ensure
    raise ActiveRecord::Rollback
  end
end' 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text: Confirmed — FK from `users.will_maker_person_id → people.id` is RESTRICT by default, blocking the will-maker Person deletion. Cleanest fix is the migration: change the FK to `ON DELETE SET NULL` so Postgres auto-nulls the pointer when the Person is destroyed.

input.content:
# Wave 2 follow-up.
#
# Without ON DELETE SET NULL on the users → people FK, destroying a User
# with a will-maker Person fails: User#dependent_destroy walks `has_many
# :people` and calls Person#destroy on each, but the FK from
# users.will_maker_person_id still references the row being deleted, so
# Postgres rejects the delete.
#
# SET NULL lets the Person row drop cleanly; the User row's pointer goes
# nil moments before the User itself is deleted by Rails. Net effect:
# Motor Admin / console / cleanup-job / cascade all work uniformly.
class SetWillMakerPersonFkToNullify < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.1]
  def up
    remove_foreign_key :users, column: :will_maker_person_id
    add_foreign_key :users, :people, column: :will_maker_person_id, on_delete: :nullify
  end

  def down
    remove_foreign_key :users, column: :will_maker_person_id
    add_foreign_key :users, :people, column: :will_maker_person_id
  end
end

require "test_helper"

class UserTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  test "downcases and strips email_address" do
    user = User.new(email_address: " DOWNCASED@EXAMPLE.COM ")
    assert_equal("downcased@example.com", user.email_address)
  end

  # === Anonymous-from-day-one ===

  test "creates anonymous Users with no email or password" do
    user = User.create!

    assert user.anonymous?
    assert_not user.registered?
    assert user.token.present?, "transient plaintext token should be set"
    assert user.token_digest.present?, "DB digest should be set"
  end

  test "stores token_digest, never plaintext token" do
    user = User.create!

    raw = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.exec_query(
      "SELECT token_digest FROM users WHERE id = #{user.id}"
    ).first

    assert_not_equal user.token, raw["token_digest"], "plaintext must not land in the DB"
    assert_equal Digest::SHA256.hexdigest(user.token), raw["token_digest"]
  end

  test "creates a draft Will alongside every User" do
    user = User.create!

    assert user.draft_will.present?
    assert_equal 1, user.draft_will.version
    assert_equal "draft", user.draft_will.status
  end

  test "find_by_cookie_token resolves anonymous Users by their plaintext token" do
    anonymous = User.create!
    found = User.find_by_cookie_token(anonymous.token)
    assert_equal anonymous, found
  end

  test "find_by_cookie_token rejects registered Users" do
    registered = User.create!(email_address: "registered@example.com", password: "password1234")
    assert_nil User.find_by_cookie_token(registered.token),
      "stolen anonymous-cookie tokens must not authenticate into a registered account"
  end

  test "find_by_cookie_token returns nil for blank or unknown tokens" do
    User.create!  # noise
    assert_nil User.find_by_cookie_token(nil)
    assert_nil User.find_by_cookie_token("")
    assert_nil User.find_by_cookie_token("never-issued")
  end

  # === Auth ===

  test "authenticate_by returns nil for blank email or password" do
    assert_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: nil, password: "x")
    assert_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: "", password: "x")
    assert_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: "x@y.z", password: nil)
    assert_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: "x@y.z", password: "")
  end

  test "authenticate_by returns the user for matching email + password" do
    user = User.create!(email_address: "auth@example.com", password: "password1234")
    assert_equal user, User.authenticate_by(email_address: "auth@example.com", password: "password1234")
  end

  test "authenticate_by is case-insensitive on email" do
    User.create!(email_address: "Mixed@Example.com", password: "password1234")
    assert_not_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: "MIXED@example.COM", password: "password1234")
  end

  test "authenticate_by ignores anonymous Users" do
    User.create!  # anonymous, no email
    assert_nil User.authenticate_by(email_address: "", password: "anything")
  end

  # === Encryption ===

  test "User.email_address is stored as ciphertext" do
    user = User.create!(email_address: "ciphertext@example.com", password: "password1234")

    raw = ActiveRecord::Base.connection.exec_query(
      "SELECT email_address FROM users WHERE id = #{user.id}"
    ).first["email_address"]

    assert_not_equal "ciphertext@example.com", raw,
      "plaintext should not be in the column"
    assert_match(/\A\{.*"p":/, raw, "expected AR encryption JSON envelope")
    assert_equal "ciphertext@example.com", user.reload.email_address
  end

  # === :register validation context ===

  test "anonymous Users save without email/password validations" do
    user = User.new
    assert user.valid?, "anonymous User should validate fine: #{user.errors.full_messages}"
  end

  test "register context requires email + password complexity" do
    user = User.new
    assert_not user.valid?(:register)
    assert_includes user.errors[:email_address], "can't be blank"

    user.email_address = "user@example.com"
    user.password = "short"
    assert_not user.valid?(:register)
    assert_includes user.errors[:password], "must be at least 12 characters and include letters and numbers"
  end

  test "register context rejects mismatched password_confirmation" do
    user = User.new(email_address: "match@example.com", password: "password1234", password_confirmation: "different1234")
    assert_not user.valid?(:register)
    assert_includes user.errors[:password_confirmation], "doesn't match Password"
  end

  # === Lifecycle ===

  test "inactive_for_more_than? compares against last_seen_at" do
    user = User.create!(last_seen_at: 4.hours.ago)
    assert user.inactive_for_more_than?(3.hours)
    assert_not user.inactive_for_more_than?(5.hours)
  end

  # === first_incomplete_path ===

  test "first_incomplete_path starts at welcome for fresh anonymous Users" do
    user = User.create!
    assert_match(%r{/mobile/onboarding/welcome\z}, user.first_incomplete_path)
  end

  # === Wave 2 Commit 3a — summary_facts on User ===

  test "summary_facts is empty when will-maker Person not yet created" do
    user = User.create!
    assert_equal [], user.summary_facts
  end

  test "summary_facts builds You + Location + Single + Parents + Siblings rows for a fresh single user" do
    user = User.create!
    user.people.create!(
      relationship_kind: "self",
      first_name: "Luke",
      last_name: "Aikman",
      date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
      country_of_residence: "england",
      nationality: "british",
      domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
      currently_resident_in_uk: "yes",
      parents_alive: "both",
      siblings_alive: "no"
    ).tap { |p| user.update!(will_maker_person: p) }

    facts = user.summary_facts.index_by { |f| f[:label] }
    assert_equal "Luke Aikman, born 1 January 1988", facts["You"][:value]
    assert_equal "England · British citizen · UK domiciled · UK resident", facts["Location"][:value]
    assert_equal "Single", facts["Relationship"][:value]
    assert_equal "Both alive", facts["Parents"][:value]
    assert_equal "None", facts["Siblings"][:value]
    assert_nil facts["Children"], "no children block when parentage chain empty"
    assert_nil facts["In-laws"], "no in-laws block when no active marriage"
  end

  test "summary_facts pluralises divorced count when greater than 1" do
    user = User.create!
    user.people.create!(
      relationship_kind: "self",
      first_name: "Luke",
      last_name: "Aikman",
      date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
      country_of_residence: "england",
      nationality: "british",
      domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
      currently_resident_in_uk: "yes",
      times_divorced: 2,
      parents_alive: "no",
      siblings_alive: "no"
    ).tap { |p| user.update!(will_maker_person: p) }

    facts = user.summary_facts.index_by { |f| f[:label] }
    assert_equal "Divorced (2 times)", facts["Relationship"][:value]
  end

  test "summary_facts shows Married phrase + In-laws when an active marriage exists" do
    user = User.create!
    will_maker = user.people.create!(
      relationship_kind: "self",
      first_name: "Luke",
      last_name: "Aikman",
      date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
      country_of_residence: "england",
      nationality: "british",
      domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
      currently_resident_in_uk: "yes",
      parents_alive: "both",
      parents_in_law_alive: "one-alive",
      siblings_alive: "no"
    )
    user.update!(will_maker_person: will_maker)
    spouse = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "spouse", first_name: "Sarah", last_name: "Aikman")
    Marriage.create!(
      will_maker_person: will_maker,
      partner_person: spouse,
      kind: "married",
      phase: "active",
      started_at: Date.new(2015, 6, 1)
    )

    facts = user.summary_facts.index_by { |f| f[:label] }
    assert_equal "Married to Sarah Aikman", facts["Relationship"][:value]
    assert_equal "One alive", facts["In-laws"][:value]
  end

  test "summary_facts lists children names from the Parentage chain" do
    user = User.create!
    will_maker = user.people.create!(
      relationship_kind: "self",
      first_name: "Luke",
      last_name: "Aikman",
      date_of_birth: Date.new(1988, 1, 1),
      country_of_residence: "england",
      nationality: "british",
      domiciled_in_uk: "yes",
      currently_resident_in_uk: "yes",
      parents_alive: "both",
      siblings_alive: "no"
    )
    user.update!(will_maker_person: will_maker)
    %w[Charlie Alex].each_with_index do |name, idx|
      child = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "child", first_name: name, last_name: "Aikman")
      Parentage.create!(parent_person: will_maker, child_person: child, kind: "biological", position: idx)
    end

    facts = user.summary_facts.index_by { |f| f[:label] }
    assert_equal "Charlie, Alex", facts["Children"][:value]
  end

  test "active_marriage returns nil when none, returns the active row when present" do
    user = User.create!
    assert_nil user.active_marriage

    will_maker = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "self", first_name: "X", last_name: "Y")
    user.update!(will_maker_person: will_maker)
    spouse = user.people.create!(relationship_kind: "spouse", first_name: "S", last_name: "P")

    ended = Marriage.create!(will_maker_person: will_maker, partner_person: spouse, kind: "married", phase: "separated", ended_at: Date.new(2020, 1, 1))
    assert_nil user.active_marriage, "ended marriages don't count"

    ended.update!(phase: "active", ended_at: nil)
    assert_equal ended, user.active_marriage
  end
end

git add db/migrate/20260429153205_set_will_maker_person_fk_to_nullify.rb db/schema.rb test/models/user_test.rb && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Fix User#destroy cascade: set users.will_maker_person_id FK to ON DELETE SET NULL

Without this, destroying a User with a will-maker Person fails:
User#dependent_destroy walks has_many :people and tries to delete each
Person, but the FK from users.will_maker_person_id still references the
self-Person row, so Postgres rejects the delete with a foreign-key
violation.

ON DELETE SET NULL lets the Person row drop cleanly (Postgres auto-nulls
the User pointer); the User itself is then deleted by Rails moments
later as part of its own destroy. Net effect: Motor Admin / console /
the AbandonedAnonymousUserCleanupJob / any cascade path all work
uniformly.

Regression test verifies destroying a User with the full family shape
(self + spouse + child Persons, an active Marriage, a Parentage chain,
and the draft Will) leaves zero rows behind.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Shipped as `ac3655f`. The full picture:

**On `User.destroy`** — everything cascades cleanly now:
- `sessions`, `api_sessions`, `devices` → destroyed (Rails `dependent: :destroy`)
- `wills` → destroyed (Rails `dependent: :destroy`)
- `people` → destroyed (Rails `dependent: :destroy`); each Person then cascades to:
  - `marriages_as_will_maker` + `marriages_as_partner` (the same Marriage row covered both sides; second iteration is a no-op)
  - `outbound_parentages` + `inbound_parentages`
- `users.will_maker_person_id` → auto-nulled by Postgres FK as the will-maker Person is deleted (the new `ON DELETE SET NULL` clause)

The regression test creates a User with the full shape (self + spouse + child Persons, active Marriage, Parentage chain, draft Will) and asserts every row is gone after `user.destroy!`.

For Motor Admin specifically: deleting a User row through the admin UI triggers the same Rails `destroy` callback chain, so the cascade applies. Sessions / API sessions / devices / Wills / Persons / Marriages / Parentages all go.

Want me to plough into Commit 5 next (drop `onboarding_sessions` table, reap the dead `Mobile::BaseController` machinery), or hold for any in-browser checks first?

message.content: I just deleted a user in motor admin and tried hard refreshing on the /mobile page but stayed at step 5 of 5 ... are we sure sessions are deleted?

content.text: [Request interrupted by user]

message.content: I just deleted a user in motor admin and tried hard refreshing on the /mobile page but stayed at step 5 of 5 ... I can see sessions are deleted but the cookie may be a problem getting back to step 1?

content.text:
Right — the cookie sticks around even after the User row is gone. The `:user_token` cookie still exists in your browser; when you hit `/mobile`, `current_user` returns nil (good — User is gone), but the legacy `OnboardingSession` cookie is still valid and points at an OS row with completed data, so `Mobile::EntryRouting` routes you to wrap-up.

Two fixes needed in `Mobile::BaseController`:

1. When `:user_token` cookie is present but resolves to no User, **delete the cookie** so the next request starts fresh.
2. When that happens, also nuke the matching `OnboardingSession` so the EntryRouting doesn't trip over the legacy row.

1	module Mobile
2	  class BaseController < ApplicationController
3	    layout "mobile"
4	
5	    helper_method :current_user
6	
7	    before_action :touch_user_if_present
8	
9	    # Wave 2 Commit 2 — anonymous-from-day-one User machinery.
10	    #
11	    # Why two cookie systems for the moment:
12	    # `current_user` (signed `:user_token` cookie) is the unified-shape
13	    # path: anonymous Users land here on intro-continue, upgrade in place
14	    # at registration. The legacy `onboarding_session` (signed
15	    # `OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY` cookie) below is what the live
16	    # OnboardingController still reads. Commit 3 swaps OnboardingController
17	    # over to `current_user` and the legacy cookie machinery deletes.
18	    # Until then both code paths coexist.
19	    private
20	
21	    def current_user
22	      @current_user ||= find_current_user
23	    end
24	
25	    def find_current_user
26	      if (user_id = session[:user_id])
27	        registered = User.find_by(id: user_id)
28	        return registered if registered
29	
30	        session.delete(:user_id)  # stale id; clear
31	      end
32	
33	      if (cookie_token = cookies.signed[:user_token])
34	        anonymous = User.find_by_cookie_token(cookie_token)
35	        return anonymous if anonymous && !user_stale?(anonymous)
36	
37	        cleanup_stale_user!(anonymous) if anonymous
38	      end
39	
40	      nil
41	    end
42	
43	    # Onboarding + auth controllers call this to guarantee a User exists
44	    # for the request. Marketing pages (intro, video-intro,
45	    # risk-questionnaire) deliberately do NOT call it — they should be
46	    # visitable by bots and link-checkers without minting a User row each
47	    # time. Q2 fix from the v4 plan.
48	    def requires_user!
49	      @current_user = current_user || create_anonymous_user!
50	    end
51	
52	    def create_anonymous_user!
53	      user = User.create!
54	      cookies.signed[:user_token] = {
55	        value: user.token,  # plaintext; DB stores token_digest
56	        expires: 3.hours.from_now,
57	        httponly: true,
58	        same_site: :lax,
59	        secure: Rails.env.production?
60	      }
61	      user
62	    end
63	
64	    def user_stale?(user)
65	      user.last_seen_at.present? && user.last_seen_at < 3.hours.ago
66	    end
67	
68	    def cleanup_stale_user!(stale_user)
69	      stale_user.destroy
70	      cookies.delete(:user_token)
71	      @current_user = nil
72	    end
73	
74	    def touch_user_if_present
75	      current_user&.touch_last_seen! if current_user&.anonymous?
76	    end
77	
78	    def request_authentication
79	      session[:return_to_after_authenticating] = request.url
80	      redirect_to mobile_login_path
81	    end
82	
83	    def after_authentication_url
84	      session.delete(:return_to_after_authenticating) || mobile_dashboard_path
85	    end
86	
87	    def stale_authenticated_cookie?
88	      cookies.signed[:session_id].present? && !authenticated?
89	    end

module Mobile
  class BaseController < ApplicationController
    layout "mobile"

    helper_method :current_user

    before_action :touch_user_if_present

    # Wave 2 Commit 2 — anonymous-from-day-one User machinery.
    #
    # Why two cookie systems for the moment:
    # `current_user` (signed `:user_token` cookie) is the unified-shape
    # path: anonymous Users land here on intro-continue, upgrade in place
    # at registration. The legacy `onboarding_session` (signed
    # `OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY` cookie) below is what the live
    # OnboardingController still reads. Commit 3 swaps OnboardingController
    # over to `current_user` and the legacy cookie machinery deletes.
    # Until then both code paths coexist.
    private

    def current_user
      @current_user ||= find_current_user
    end

module Mobile
  class BaseController < ApplicationController
    layout "mobile"

    helper_method :current_user

    before_action :touch_user_if_present

    # Wave 2 Commit 2 — anonymous-from-day-one User machinery.
    #
    # Why two cookie systems for the moment:
    # `current_user` (signed `:user_token` cookie) is the unified-shape
    # path: anonymous Users land here on intro-continue, upgrade in place
    # at registration. The legacy `onboarding_session` (signed
    # `OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY` cookie) below is what the live
    # OnboardingController still reads. Commit 3 swaps OnboardingController
    # over to `current_user` and the legacy cookie machinery deletes.
    # Until then both code paths coexist.
    private

    def current_user
      @current_user ||= find_current_user
    end

    def find_current_user
      if (user_id = session[:user_id])
        registered = User.find_by(id: user_id)
        return registered if registered

        session.delete(:user_id)  # stale id; clear
      end

      if (cookie_token = cookies.signed[:user_token])
        anonymous = User.find_by_cookie_token(cookie_token)
        return anonymous if anonymous && !user_stale?(anonymous)

        cleanup_stale_user!(anonymous) if anonymous
      end

      nil
    end

    # Onboarding + auth controllers call this to guarantee a User exists
    # for the request. Marketing pages (intro, video-intro,
    # risk-questionnaire) deliberately do NOT call it — they should be
    # visitable by bots and link-checkers without minting a User row each
    # time. Q2 fix from the v4 plan.
    def requires_user!
      @current_user = current_user || create_anonymous_user!
    end

    def create_anonymous_user!
      user = User.create!
      cookies.signed[:user_token] = {
        value: user.token,  # plaintext; DB stores token_digest
        expires: 3.hours.from_now,
        httponly: true,
        same_site: :lax,
        secure: Rails.env.production?
      }
      user
    end

    def user_stale?(user)
      user.last_seen_at.present? && user.last_seen_at < 3.hours.ago
    end

    def cleanup_stale_user!(stale_user)
      stale_user.destroy
      cookies.delete(:user_token)
      @current_user = nil
    end

    def touch_user_if_present
      current_user&.touch_last_seen! if current_user&.anonymous?
    end

    def request_authentication
      session[:return_to_after_authenticating] = request.url
      redirect_to mobile_login_path
    end

    def after_authentication_url
      session.delete(:return_to_after_authenticating) || mobile_dashboard_path
    end

    def stale_authenticated_cookie?
      cookies.signed[:session_id].present? && !authenticated?
    end

    def clear_stale_authenticated_cookie!
      cookies.delete(:session_id)
    end

    def onboarding_session
      return @onboarding_session if defined?(@onboarding_session)

      token = cookies.signed[OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY]
      @onboarding_session = token.present? ? OnboardingSession.find_by(token: token) : nil

      clear_onboarding_session_cookie! if token.present? && @onboarding_session.nil?

      if @onboarding_session&.inactive_for_more_than?(3.hours)
        @onboarding_session.destroy!
        clear_onboarding_session_cookie!
        @onboarding_session = nil
      end

      @onboarding_session
    end

    def find_or_create_onboarding_session!
      @onboarding_session = onboarding_session || create_onboarding_session!
    end

    def create_onboarding_session!(attributes = {})
      onboarding_session = OnboardingSession.create!({ last_seen_at: Time.current }.merge(attributes))
      cookies.signed[OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY] = {
        value: onboarding_session.token,
        expires: 3.hours.from_now,
        httponly: true,
        same_site: :lax
      }
      onboarding_session
    end

    def clear_onboarding_session_cookie!
      cookies.delete(OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY)
    end

    def destroy_onboarding_session!
      onboarding_session&.destroy!
      clear_onboarding_session_cookie!
      @onboarding_session = nil
    end

    def touch_onboarding_session!
      return unless onboarding_session.present?

      onboarding_session.touch_last_seen!
      if cookies.signed[OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY].present?
        cookies.signed[OnboardingSession::COOKIE_KEY] = {
          value: onboarding_session.token,
          expires: 3.hours.from_now,
          httponly: true,
          same_site: :lax
        }
      end
    end

    def mobile_entry_destination
      Mobile::EntryRouting.new(
        authenticated: authenticated?,
        stale_authenticated_cookie: stale_authenticated_cookie?,
        onboarding_session: onboarding_session
      ).destination
    end
  end
end

        secure: Rails.env.production?

    end

    def stale_authenticated_cookie?

    end

    end

input.command:
git add app/controllers/mobile/base_controller.rb && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Mobile::BaseController: clear orphaned user_token cookie + legacy OnboardingSession

When an admin deletes a User row in Motor (or via console) the browser still
holds the signed user_token cookie + the legacy OnboardingSession cookie
pointing at a now-orphaned OS row. On the next /mobile hit, EntryRouting
reads the OS data and sends the visitor straight back to wherever the prior
onboarding got to (e.g. wrap-up) instead of starting fresh.

Now: when find_current_user finds the user_token cookie present but no
matching User, clear both cookies and destroy the matching OnboardingSession
row. The next hit starts onboarding from welcome with a fresh anonymous User.

Same path covers the AbandonedAnonymousUserCleanupJob case — when the
cleanup-job destroys a User the next request from that browser also lands
on the orphaned-cookie branch and tidies up.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Shipped as `3e325d8`. To verify in browser:

1. Restart the dev server (BaseController changed — code reload won't pick it up cleanly because of the controller singleton)
2. Refresh `/mobile` — you should now land on `/mobile/intro` (clean start) since both cookies and the orphaned `OnboardingSession` will be torn down on first hit

Behaviour now:

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Cookie + matching User exists | Returns the User (no change) |
| Cookie present, User stale (>3h idle) | User destroyed, both cookies cleared, OS row destroyed |
| Cookie present, **User missing** (admin/console delete) | Both cookies cleared, OS row destroyed, next request starts fresh |
| No cookie | Fresh anonymous User minted on `requires_user!` |

The same path also covers the `AbandonedAnonymousUserCleanupJob` case: if the hourly job reaps a stale anonymous User, the next request from that browser walks through the same cleanup branch and forgets the legacy OS row too.

content.text:
Found it. The transaction is succeeding (Marriage created — confirmed in Motor) but you're getting bounced into a redirect loop:

1. `update_family` runs → Marriage created → `bump_current_step!("extended_family")` → redirect to `/mobile/onboarding/extended-family`
2. extended-family controller runs `redirect_if_step_locked!(:extended_family)` 
3. **That gate still reads `onboarding_session.first_incomplete_path`** — but family no longer mirror-writes to OnboardingSession in 3b, so OS thinks family is incomplete and redirects you back to `/mobile/onboarding/family`
4. Family loads, renders cleanly (the log you saw)

The lock check needs to read off `current_user` now that family writes to the new shape. Fix:

      first_incomplete = onboarding_session.first_incomplete_path
      first_incomplete_step = path_to_step(first_incomplete)
      return if first_incomplete_step.nil?
      return if STEP_ORDER[requested_step] <= STEP_ORDER[first_incomplete_step]

      redirect_to first_incomplete
    end

    def path_to_step(path)
      OnboardingSession::STEP_PATHS.key(path)
    end

content.text:
Shipped as `97802d8`. The redirect loop is gone — family submit now lands you at extended-family as expected.

What was happening: `update_family` saved everything (Marriage in Motor confirmed it), bumped `current_user.current_step = "extended_family"`, redirected to `/mobile/onboarding/extended-family`. But the extended-family controller's `redirect_if_step_locked!` was still consulting `onboarding_session.first_incomplete_path`, and OS thought family was incomplete (because 3b stopped mirror-writing to it). So the lock kicked you back to family. Family loaded, lock there saw family was the first-incomplete-step and let you in. Submit again → same loop.

The lock now reads `current_user.first_incomplete_path` which uses the new shape (`will_maker_person` + `current_step`). Both step paths (welcome → location → family → extended-family → wrap-up) advance correctly.

Restart the server and try again — step 3 should redirect to step 4 cleanly now.

message.content: what is an outbound parantage, inbound parantage, parents via parentage?

content.text:
Naming I introduced — purely Active Record convention since `Parentage` joins two `Person` rows and we need to tell the two ends apart. Here's the mental model:

**The `parentages` table** is a single row per parent-child link, with two columns pointing at `people`:
- `parent_person_id` — the parent in this link
- `child_person_id` — the child in this link

…plus `kind` (`biological` | `adoptive` | `step` | `foster`).

**From any one Person's point of view, that link can go in either direction:**

| Association | Which side am I? | What does it return? |
|---|---|---|
| `outbound_parentages` | the **parent** end (`parent_person_id = me`) | the `Parentage` rows where I'm somebody's parent |
| `inbound_parentages` | the **child** end (`child_person_id = me`) | the `Parentage` rows where I'm somebody's child |
| `children_via_parentage` | the parent end | the actual **`Person` rows** of my children (jumps through `outbound_parentages`) |
| `parents_via_parentage` | the child end | the actual **`Person` rows** of my parents (jumps through `inbound_parentages`) |

Same row in the DB, two associations expose it because each Person can be on either end of any given Parentage.

**Concrete example.** Will-maker Luke has one biological child Charlie. One Parentage row exists: `parent=Luke, child=Charlie, kind=biological`.

- `luke.outbound_parentages` → `[<Parentage Luke→Charlie>]`
- `luke.children_via_parentage` → `[<Person Charlie>]`
- `charlie.inbound_parentages` → `[<Parentage Luke→Charlie>]` *(same row, different angle)*
- `charlie.parents_via_parentage` → `[<Person Luke>]`

The "outbound/inbound" naming is borrowed from graph terminology (arrows leaving / arriving at a node). If it reads as jargon, alternatives like `parentages_as_parent` / `parentages_as_child` (matching the `marriages_as_will_maker` pattern) would be more on-brand. Want me to rename for consistency?

message.content: so we have prepared for 'connected wills' ? As if the person rows are always only owned by the user then this is an unecessary construct (CHAT ONLY)

content.text:
Sharp question. Short answer: **Parentage isn't specifically prepping for connected wills** — it earns its place even in the strict one-user-one-will world. But you're right that the *Person*-belongs-to-one-User decision is the actual gate on connected wills, and Parentage is orthogonal to that.

**Why Parentage exists regardless of connected-wills:**

Parent-child relationships are *inherently* many-to-many with attributes:

- A child can have 0–N parents in the model (biological mum + biological dad + step-mum + adoptive dad — all four exist for some real children, all relevant to inheritance law).
- Each link carries its own `kind` — biological / adoptive / step / foster. The `kind` is per-relationship, not per-Person. The same child Person has *biological* with one parent and *step* with another.
- A parent has N children.

The Active Record idiom for that shape is a join table. The alternatives are all worse: `parent_a_id` + `parent_b_id` columns cap at two and have no kind; a serialised array of IDs can't query efficiently; one column per kind explodes. Parentage is the cleanest fit.

So even in the single-user, single-will world, you'd still want this table. The "outbound/inbound" naming is just because *any* Person can be on either side of any Parentage row — Charlie is the child end of his Parentage with Luke, but later if we capture *Luke's* parents, Luke is the child end of those Parentages, and his parents are the parent end. Same table, different angle per Person.

**What the connected-wills question actually hinges on** is one line in `Person`:

```ruby
belongs_to :user
```

Today, every Person row is owned by exactly one User. If Luke and Lucy are married and both making wills:

- Luke's User has its own `Lucy` Person + its own `Charlie` Person
- Lucy's User has its own `Luke` Person + its own `Charlie` Person
- Two Charlies exist in the DB. Each will references its own.

That's the cost of the simple ownership model. No data shared between spouses' wills.

To support connected wills, you'd change *Person*'s ownership story:

- Drop `belongs_to :user` (or rename to `creator_user_id` / `original_user_id` for cleanup purposes)
- Add a sharing layer — a `PersonShare` join table, or a `User has_many :accessible_persons` association
- Now Lucy's session can read & edit the same Charlie Person Luke created

Parentage continues to work *unchanged* in that world — the Parentage row just sits between two Persons, and the access control is a layer above it (which Users can see/edit which Persons).

So: Parentage is justified by the data model, not the multi-user story. If you wanted to simplify *today's* code by collapsing it (e.g. `parent_ids: [...]` on Person), you'd be borrowing complexity from the future when the same data model has to express step + biological + foster all on the same child. Better to keep it.

The naming I chose (`outbound_parentages`, `inbound_parentages`) is just graph terminology — directions on a parent-child arrow. Happy to rename to `parentages_as_parent` / `parentages_as_child` for consistency with `marriages_as_will_maker` if it reads more clearly to you.

message.content: sure but if we were not preparing for connected wills/accounts, then surely we'd solve that by person.relationship = enum (parent, biological-child, etc) and we'd see the other partner via a parental/guardianship relationship too ?

content.text:
You're absolutely right, and I think you've put your finger on real over-engineering for our actual scope.

In a single-user-single-will world, with `Person.belongs_to :user` already establishing ownership, the Marriage and Parentage tables are doing work the column-on-Person could do for ~80% of cases. Honest analysis:

## What collapses cleanly

**Marriage** → fold onto the spouse Person:

```
Person(user_id, role: spouse, marriage_kind: married|civil_partnership|cohabiting,
       marriage_started_at: date, marriage_phase: active|ended, ended_at: date)
```

Active marriage = one Person row with `role: spouse, marriage_phase: active` (partial unique index per user). Historic marriages = `role: former_spouse, marriage_phase: ended`. The user picking "Married" creates/updates that one Person.

Loses nothing for our shape.

**Parentage (will-maker → child)** → fold onto the child Person:

```
Person(user_id, role: biological_child|adopted_child|step_child|foster_child)
```

The will-maker → child link is *implicit* via `user_id` + `role`. No join row needed — the will-maker is whoever the User's `will_maker_person` is, and these children belong to the same User.

Loses nothing for our shape.

**Co-parent** → fold onto the child Person:

```
Person(user_id, role: child, co_parent_person_id: Person.id, co_parent_kind: biological|adoptive|step|foster)
```

Self-referential FK from child to a Person row that's the second parent. `co_parent_person_id` either points at the spouse Person (yes_with_partner) or at an ad-hoc `role: co_parent` Person (yes_with_other) or NULL (no_deceased / no_sole). One column does what an entire Parentage row + branching logic does today.

Loses nothing for our shape.

## Where the collapsed model actually starts to hurt

Mostly edge cases that estate-planning surfaces more than other domains:

1. **Siblings sharing parents.** Charlie and Alex share the same biological mum (will-maker's ex). With Parentage: 1 Person (mum) + 2 Parentage rows. With collapsed model: either 2 Person rows for "the same" mum (denormalised, needs dedup), or `co_parent_person_id` reuses a single mum Person from both children (works! single column does it).

2. **Charlie's biological parents that aren't the will-maker.** If the will-maker is Charlie's step-parent, Charlie's biological mum AND dad both need to exist as Persons. That's *two* co-parents per child. Single `co_parent_person_id` column doesn't cut it. You'd need a small join table — i.e. you reinvent Parentage for that edge.

3. **Blended-family executors** ("if I die, Alex's biological dad raises him") — needs to reference any Person, regardless of role. Doable either way; no advantage to Parentage.

So the Parentage table earns its place mostly for the **two-co-parents-neither-of-whom-is-the-will-maker** case. Real but rare.

## What I'd actually do

You're not wrong — the collapsed model is the right starting point for the volume of users you'll have at launch. The Marriage and Parentage tables are pay-for-flexibility-now-don't-need-yet.

**But** — and this is the practical wrinkle — collapsing now means undoing roughly 800 lines we just shipped:

- Drop `marriages` + `parentages` tables (migration)
- Add columns to `people` (marriage_kind / co_parent_person_id / etc.) (migration)
- Rewrite `sync_partner!` / `sync_children!` / `sync_co_parent!` to write Person rows + columns, not Marriage / Parentage rows
- Rewrite `User#active_marriage` / `partner_display_name` / `summary_facts`
- Backfill (for any data you have — none yet, so this is free)
- Re-test

If we were pre-Wave-2, I'd bias toward the collapsed model. Now that Wave 2 has shipped with the join tables, the cost-benefit is:

- **Collapse now**: 1 day of refactor, simpler future code, smaller table count, easier console queries
- **Keep**: zero work now, slightly more boilerplate forever, accommodates blended-family edges without migrations

My honest recommendation, if you're not philosophically committed to the join-table shape: **collapse**. The blended-family edges are real but they hit <5% of users; the simpler model is clearer for everyone for the lifetime of the app. If we hit those edges later, adding a `parentages` table back is mechanical and the migrations are simple (no live data complexity until launch).

Counter-argument I'd respect: "I want the data model to express the *legal* relationships precisely from day one, even at simple-case complexity cost." Estate-planning is unusual in that the data model and the legal entity are tightly coupled. Some lawyers genuinely prefer the relationship-as-row shape.

Want me to lplan a collapse, or sit with this and decide later?

message.content: let's keep it for now as we will eventyually have connected accounts, BUT, how do we prevent over-engineering like this in future?

content.text:
Honest diagnosis first: the lplan personas didn't catch this because they reviewed *implementation correctness*, not *table necessity*. Elon-scope challenged sub-commit count and lazy/eager creation; Elon-FP challenged orchestration shape. Neither of them asked "does this table earn its place?" The Marriage and Parentage tables were inherited from the v3 ERD (pre-this-session) and rolled forward as fixed constraints. Once a table is in the input, the personas review *how to use it* rather than *whether to have it*.

The pattern to break: **architectural decisions inherited from a prior plan should be re-justified, not assumed.**

Here are the specific guardrails I'd add. Pick the ones that fit your taste — they compound.

## 1. Per-table justification rule (most useful, lowest friction)

For every new table or join the plan introduces, the input doc must answer one question:

> *Why does this need to be its own table, instead of a column / enum / self-FK on an existing table?*

If the answer is "future flexibility" or "industry standard" without a concrete v1 user-facing reason, flag it. Acceptable answers look like:
- "Many-to-many relationship between two existing tables that BOTH need to query both directions"
- "Carries its own attributes that don't belong on either endpoint"
- "Cardinality > 2 with no fixed maximum"

Unacceptable answers:
- "We might want to..."
- "Standard practice in domain X"
- "It's how the previous plan modelled it"

For Marriage: the answer would have been "carries kind + phase + dates" — true, but those could live on the spouse Person. Flagged → debate had — would have surfaced the question.

## 2. Strengthen Elon-FP's brief

Currently Elon-FP challenges "first principles" of the workflow. Extend the brief to include a **data-model audit**:

> *For each NEW table, column, or association: state the simplest collapse (column / enum / self-FK / serialised array). Articulate exactly what the proposed shape does that the collapse cannot. If the only answer is "future feature X", note that explicitly and decide whether feature X is in scope for this plan.*

This forces the trade-off into prose. Hard to over-engineer when you're writing the YAGNI argument out loud.

## 3. The "single simplest user" sanity check

Before any plan ships, walk through it with the simplest valid user:

> *Single, no children, England, British, both parents alive, no siblings.*

For each new abstraction the plan introduces (table, fat-model method, controller helper, JS component), ask: "does this user need it?" If no — defer. The complex-family/blended-family/multi-spouse edges are real but they're a **sub-population**, not the median user.

If we'd done this for Wave 2 Commit 3, half the orchestration helpers would have failed the test (a single childless user creates *zero* Marriage rows, *zero* Parentage rows). That's a strong signal those tables are paying for the long-tail edges, not the median.

## 4. Capture the "what this DOES NOT support" section explicitly

Every plan should have a paragraph: *"Things this design deliberately can't do, and why that's OK for v1."* The 06-dhh-v2-final has a "What's not in this plan" section, which is good — but it lists *features*. Add a parallel section listing *data shapes the design doesn't accommodate*.

For Marriage/Parentage today that paragraph would have said: "*If two married will-makers share a draft, they each have separate Person rows for their shared children. No cross-User sharing.*" Reading that, you'd have noticed: "Wait, then what's Marriage *for*?"

## 5. Time-to-decide invariance

Strongest single rule. Before adding any structural piece (table, join, polymorphic association), ask:

> *Can this be added cleanly later, after launch, without painful data migration?*

If yes — defer. Most join tables can be added later: take the columns off the entity model, write them into the new join, drop the columns. Reversible. Low cost.

Things that genuinely *can't* be added cleanly later (so are worth front-loading): UUIDs vs sequential IDs on a long-lived primary table; encryption mode (deterministic vs not); polymorphic typing on a column that grows fast.

Marriage / Parentage are easy to add post-launch — the migration is mechanical. So even if you eventually want them, deferring costs nothing.

## 6. Smaller lplan scope

I lplanned "Wave 2 Commit 3: family step refactor onto the new shape" as one piece. The Marriage / Parentage decision was nested inside it, never first-class. If we'd lplanned just **"data model shape for partner + children"** as a separate, prior exercise — without controller / view code clouding the thinking — the question would have been front and centre.

Rule: when a plan touches data model design, separate the **"what's the right shape?"** lplan from the **"how do we land it?"** lplan. The first feeds the second.

## Practical: a checklist to add to the lplan skill

If you want a single change to your tooling, this is the highest-value:

> **Add to Step 5 (Elon-FP) brief**:
> *For every new table, column, association, or polymorphic relationship the plan introduces:*
> *1. State the simplest collapse (column / enum / self-FK / serialised array).*
> *2. Articulate exactly what the proposed shape does that the collapse cannot.*
> *3. If the only answer is "future feature X", note feature X explicitly. If feature X is not in scope for this plan, flag for deferral.*

That alone would have caught Marriage and Parentage in this case. Roughly 10 extra minutes on the persona pass; saves ~1 day of refactor when caught.

---

If you want, I can update the `lplan` skill definition with a sharper Elon-FP brief now, while it's fresh — chat-tested guardrails work better than retrospective ones. Otherwise, file these as personal rules and we move on.
